attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old man of Japanese ethnicity presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine check-up. He has a medical history of hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia. His medications are perindopril and atorvastatin. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally.

On examination, he appears well with a pulse rate of 74/min, blood pressure 138/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His body mass index is 25 kg/m².

Recent blood tests show:

  • Total cholesterol 5.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.5)
  • High-density lipoprotein cholesterol 1.2 mmol/L (1.0-2.0)
  • Fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.5)

You use the Australian cardiovascular disease risk calculator and estimate his absolute risk of a cardiovascular event in the next five years as 5%.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is a reclassification factor that applies to this patient's cardiovascular risk assessment?
options: Body mass index of 25 kg/m² warrants consideration for reclassification
Male sex warrants consideration for reclassification to high risk
Age over 55 years warrants consideration for reclassification to high risk
Japanese ethnicity warrants consideration for reclassification to low risk
Age under 60 years warrants consideration for reclassification to low risk
attributes.explanation:
The Australian Absolute Cardiovascular Disease Risk Calculator (AusCVDRisk) is designed to estimate the absolute risk of a cardiovascular event over a five-year period. Risk categories are defined as:

  • high (≥10% risk over five years)

  • intermediate (5 to <10% risk over five years)

  • low (<5% risk over five years).

However, reclassification factors that should be considered include ethnicity, chronic kidney disease, severe mental illness, coronary artery calcium score, and family history of premature cardiovascular disease. Consideration for reclassification should be based on these factors when individuals fall closer to the threshold for another risk category.

Japanese ethnicity warrants consideration for reclassification to low risk is correct. People of East Asian background (including Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Taiwanese, or Mongolian) have a lower baseline risk of cardiovascular disease compared to the general Australian population. Current guidelines recommend reclassifying these groups to low risk when using the Australian cardiovascular disease risk calculator, provided they fall close to the threshold for a lower risk category. In this case, with a calculated risk of 5% (at the lower boundary of intermediate risk), reclassification to low risk (<5%) should be considered.

Body mass index (BMI) of 25 kg/m² warrants consideration for reclassification is incorrect. While obesity is a risk factor for cardiovascular disease, BMI is not listed as a specific reclassification factor in the Australian guidelines.

Male sex warrants consideration for reclassification to high risk is incorrect. Sex is already incorporated into the standard cardiovascular risk calculator and does not require additional reclassification.

Age over 55 years warrants consideration for reclassification to high risk and Age under 60 years warrants consideration for reclassification to low risk are both incorrect. Age is a fundamental component of the cardiovascular risk calculator and is already accounted for in the initial calculation. The calculator already accounts for the continuous relationship between age and cardiovascular risk.

In contrast to East Asian ethnicities, it is recommended to consider reclassifying estimated CVD risk to high risk for individuals of Indigenous Australian, Māori, Pacific Islander, or South Asian descent (Indian, Pakistani, Bangladeshi, Sri Lankan, Nepali, Bhutanese, Maldivian ethnicities) if their calculated risk is close to the threshold for a higher risk category.
learningPoints: In the Australian cardiovascular disease risk calculator, reclassification factors include ethnicity, chronic kidney disease, severe mental illness, coronary artery calcium score, and family history of premature cardiovascular disease.

Individuals of East Asian ethnicity (Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Taiwanese, or Mongolian) may be considered for reclassification to low risk when their risk score is close to the threshold for this category.
references: Nelson MR, Banks E, Brown A, Chow CK, Peiris DP, Stocks NP, Davies Ao R, Raffoul N, Kalman L, Bradburn E, Jennings G. 2023 Australian guideline for assessing and managing cardiovascular disease risk. Med J Aust. 2024 May 20;220(9):482-490. Available from: https://www.mja.com.au/journal/2024/220/9/2023-australian-guideline-assessing-and-managing-cardiovascular-disease-risk

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
